parents.father_research_notes: Son of Daniel Sutton and Martha Goodall Sutton.

bio.bio_blurb: Wyoming Valley pioneer and mill owner

burial.plot_notes: Hollenback Cemetery, Wilkes-Barre PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: A creek in the Wyoming Valley bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: A merchant at North Castle, New York, James Sutton sold out at the Revolution's onset and removed to Exeter in the Wyoming Valley, building a saw and grist mill on a stream named for him, Sutton's Creek.

narrative_blocks.header: He guarded the women and children at Forty Fort
narrative_blocks.body: A Quaker scrupulous about bearing arms, Sutton was nonetheless stationed inside Forty Fort on the day of the 1778 Wyoming Massacre, charged with protecting the women, children, and aged who sheltered there.

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Daniel Sutton and Martha Goodall Sutton.

burial.plot_notes: Hollenback Cemetery, Wilkes-Barre PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.body: A merchant at North Castle, New York, James Sutton sold out at the Revolution's onset and removed to Exeter in the Wyoming Valley, building a saw and grist mill on a stream named for him, Sutton's Creek.

burial.plot_notes: Hollenback Cemetery, Wilkes-Barre PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: She followed her father to the Wyoming Valley
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Smith married James Sutton and removed with him and her father, Dr. William Hooker Smith, to the Wyoming Valley of Pennsylvania, settling at Exeter where Sutton's mills stood.

cross_connections.display_label: was a militia surgeon at the 1778 Wyoming Massacre

narrative_blocks.header: A Wyoming Massacre survivor who reached ninety-six
narrative_blocks.body: Born in 1773, Deborah Sutton was a small child at the Wyoming settlement during the 1778 raid and survived it, living on to the age of ninety-six in the Pennsylvania valley her family had helped settle.

bio.title: Dr.
bio.bio_blurb: Physician and Civil War militia private

burial.plot_notes: Delta Cemetery, Westernville, Oneida County NY. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.body: Polly Sutton Catlin sat for a portrait by her son, the painter George Catlin; the picture is now held by the Smithsonian American Art Museum.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of fourteen, including a famous painter
narrative_blocks.body: Raised in the Wyoming Valley by pioneer settlers, Polly married the lawyer Putnam Catlin in 1789 and raised fourteen children — the fifth of whom was the artist George Catlin.

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Eli Catlin of Litchfield, Connecticut.

bio.bio_blurb: Wilkes-Barre lawyer and Revolutionary fife major

documents.document_blurb: His 1815 letter weighing whether to fund son George at the Litchfield Law School.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, Hallstead, Susquehanna County PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: He was a fife major in the Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: As a youth Putnam Catlin served as a drummer and fife major in the 2nd Connecticut Regiment during the Revolutionary War (DAR Patriot A022901).

narrative_blocks.header: A Wilkes-Barre lawyer who moved up the Susquehanna
narrative_blocks.body: Admitted to the bar in 1786, Catlin practiced law at Wilkes-Barre before failing health led him to farm along the upper Susquehanna, serving as postmaster at Hopbottom.

notable.notable_blurb: Painter of Native American life and the frontier

cross_connections.display_label: was a portrait painter who toured Europe alongside him

documents.document_blurb: His father's 1815 letter on possibly sending him to study law at Litchfield.

burial.plot_notes: Green-Wood Cemetery, Brooklyn (Kings County) NY. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: He painted hundreds of Native American portraits
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1830s George Catlin journeyed across the American West, painting more than five hundred portraits and scenes of Native life among dozens of tribes.

narrative_blocks.header: His Indian Gallery toured America and Europe
narrative_blocks.body: Catlin gathered his works into a traveling "Indian Gallery" shown across the United States and in London and Paris, while he argued for the preservation of Native cultures.
A large body of Catlin's work is held today by the Smithsonian American Art Museum, among the fullest visual records of Plains peoples before mid-century.

narrative_blocks.header: He drowned while sketching the falls
narrative_blocks.body: Julius Catlin, a brother of the painter and himself of artistic bent, drowned in 1828 at Rochester, New York, while sketching the falls there, at about twenty-four.

narrative_blocks.header: He drowned crossing the Susquehanna in 1842
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Sutton farmed, milled and lumbered the family homestead at Exeter until 1842, when the skiff he was crossing the Susquehanna in went down and he and a companion were lost.

bio.bio_blurb: Union private wounded at First Bull Run

military_service.notes: Enlisted 29 Apr 1861; wounded at the First Battle of Bull Run, July 1861; mustered out 3 May 1864.

narrative_blocks.header: He took a wound at First Bull Run
narrative_blocks.body: A lumber clerk who enlisted with the 1st Minnesota in 1861, Stephen Sutton was wounded at the First Battle of Bull Run and served until 1864, later settling as a Minneapolis merchant.

narrative_blocks.header: The Exeter farm boy who became mayor
narrative_blocks.body: One of twin sons raised on the Exeter homestead, Charles B. Sutton went into business in Wilkes-Barre and was elected mayor of the city.

career.notes: State contracts for canals and locks on the Rock River

bio.title: Major
bio.bio_blurb: Canal contractor; namesake of Sterling, Illinois

narrative_blocks.header: An Illinois city was named after him
narrative_blocks.body: While running the public works on the Rock River in 1836, James Sterling was honored when settlers chose his name for their new village, today the city of Sterling, Illinois.

narrative_blocks.header: He befriended Lincoln and lived to elect him
narrative_blocks.body: Sterling struck up a lasting friendship with Abraham Lincoln, and counted among the last satisfactions of his life voting for Lincoln and living to see him elected president in 1860.

burialCemetery.name: Hollenback Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Hollenback Cemetery, Wilkes-Barre, Luzerne County, Pennsylvania.
